labs.title: EU AI Act Risk Classification Workshop
labs.description: Classify 5 AI systems using EU AI Act risk tiers and map applicable obligations
steps: Step 1: Review the 5 AI system descriptions (credit scoring, medical imaging, recruitment screener, smart CCTV, chatbot)
Step 2: Apply EU AI Act Annex III high-risk criteria to each system
Step 3: Classify each as Unacceptable / High / Limited / Minimal risk with justification
Step 4: Map specific obligations for each High-risk system (technical documentation, conformity assessment, human oversight)
Step 5: Identify the 2 systems most urgently requiring governance intervention
Step 6: Draft a one-page executive briefing summarising findings

labs.title: Bias Detection in a Hiring AI Model
labs.description: Use Fairlearn to detect and quantify demographic bias in a pre-trained hiring model
steps: Step 1: Load the provided hiring model and dataset (1,000 applicants with 3 protected attributes)
Step 2: Calculate selection rate by demographic group
Step 3: Compute disparate impact ratio (80% rule threshold)
Step 4: Generate Fairlearn MetricFrame comparing accuracy, precision, and recall across groups
Step 5: Apply GridSearch mitigation technique (equalized odds) and re-evaluate
Step 6: Document findings in audit workpaper format with severity rating
Step 7: Recommend governance controls to prevent future bias introduction

practice_questions.text: Under the EU AI Act, which type of AI system is classified as 'Unacceptable Risk' and must be prohibited?
options: AI-powered spam filters
Real-time remote biometric identification systems in public spaces by law enforcement (with narrow exceptions)
AI chatbots for customer service
AI systems that recommend products based on browsing history
practice_questions.explanation: The EU AI Act (Art. 5) prohibits real-time remote biometric identification in public spaces by law enforcement, with very narrow exceptions (imminent terrorist threats). It also bans social scoring, AI exploiting vulnerabilities, and subliminal manipulation. Product recommendation AI falls in the 'Minimal Risk' category.

practice_questions.scenario: A financial institution's credit-scoring AI shows a loan approval rate of 72% for White applicants and 48% for Black applicants, with similar creditworthiness profiles.
practice_questions.text: Using the 80% rule (disparate impact ratio), what is the finding and recommended immediate action?
options: No issue — approval rates over 40% are acceptable
Disparate impact confirmed (ratio = 0.67, below 0.8 threshold) — suspend model pending bias audit and root-cause analysis
Suspend all lending operations immediately
Report only if the gap exceeds 50 percentage points
practice_questions.explanation: Disparate impact ratio = 48/72 = 0.667, well below the 0.8 (80%) threshold established by EEOC and widely adopted in AI fairness assessment. The model should be suspended for a full bias audit covering training data, feature selection, and model architecture before re-deployment.

practice_questions.text: Which NIST AI RMF function focuses on identifying and prioritising AI risks in context with organisational strategy?
options: Govern
Map
Measure
Manage
practice_questions.explanation: The 'Map' function of the NIST AI RMF focuses on context and risk identification — categorising AI systems, identifying stakeholders, and mapping risks to organisational objectives. 'Govern' sets policies; 'Measure' quantifies risk; 'Manage' implements controls and responses.

labs.title: Cloud Shared Responsibility Mapping
labs.description: Map security responsibilities across IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS for 3 real cloud architectures
steps: Step 1: Review the 3 cloud architectures provided (IaaS web server, PaaS app, SaaS CRM)
Step 2: Using the CSA CCM, identify all security control domains applicable to each
Step 3: For each control, assign responsibility: Cloud Provider / Customer / Shared
Step 4: Identify the 3 controls where customers most commonly make incorrect assumptions
Step 5: Draft a Shared Responsibility Matrix for the IaaS architecture
Step 6: Identify audit evidence sources for each customer-owned control

labs.title: Cloud Security Posture Assessment
labs.description: Use CSPM concepts to evaluate a cloud environment against CIS Benchmarks
steps: Step 1: Review the provided cloud environment configuration snapshots (AWS/Azure)
Step 2: Map configurations against CIS Benchmark Level 1 controls
Step 3: Identify non-compliant settings (open S3 buckets, unrestricted security groups, MFA gaps)
Step 4: Score the environment: compliant vs. non-compliant control count
Step 5: Prioritise findings by risk (Critical/High/Medium/Low)
Step 6: Draft remediation recommendations with estimated effort
Step 7: Create a 30-day remediation roadmap for Critical findings

practice_questions.text: In a cloud Shared Responsibility Model (IaaS), which security control is ALWAYS the customer's responsibility regardless of cloud provider?
options: Physical data centre security
Hypervisor patching
Customer data classification and access control
Network backbone reliability
practice_questions.explanation: In IaaS, the customer always owns: data classification, access controls to their data, operating system patching, and application security. The cloud provider owns physical security, hypervisor, and network infrastructure. This boundary shifts in PaaS (provider also owns OS) and SaaS (provider owns nearly everything except user data and access).

practice_questions.scenario: Your organisation stores EU citizen data in AWS S3 buckets in us-east-1. The legal team has confirmed no Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) are in place.
practice_questions.text: What is the MOST critical compliance risk and immediate remediation action?
options: No risk — US cloud providers are GDPR-exempt
GDPR Chapter V violation (cross-border transfer without safeguards) — immediately implement SCCs or migrate data to an EU region
Data should be encrypted with AES-256 to comply
Notify AWS to apply GDPR settings in their console
practice_questions.explanation: GDPR Chapter V prohibits transfers of EU personal data to third countries without adequate safeguards. The invalidation of Privacy Shield (Schrems II) means SCCs are the primary mechanism. Without SCCs or an adequacy decision, each transfer is an active violation. Immediate remediation: sign SCCs with AWS Data Processing Addendum and/or migrate to AWS EU regions.

practice_questions.text: Which Zero Trust principle is MOST directly violated when a cloud environment uses a 'flat' network with no micro-segmentation between workloads?
options: Verify explicitly
Use least privilege access
Assume breach — limit blast radius
Inspect and log all traffic
practice_questions.explanation: The 'Assume Breach' principle requires limiting the blast radius through micro-segmentation, so that a compromised workload cannot laterally move to other systems. A flat network allows unrestricted east-west traffic, enabling an attacker who compromises one VM to reach all others. Micro-segmentation (VPC subnets, NSGs, service meshes) is the primary control.

labs.title: Incident Response Plan Development
labs.description: Build a complete IR plan for a ransomware scenario at a financial institution
steps: Step 1: Define IR team structure, roles, and contact tree
Step 2: Create incident classification matrix (P1-P4 severity levels)
Step 3: Write detection and initial triage procedures for ransomware indicators
Step 4: Document containment playbook (network isolation, credential reset)
Step 5: Draft stakeholder notification templates (CEO, Board, Regulators)
Step 6: Design recovery procedures and RTO/RPO targets

practice_questions.scenario: Your organisation's risk appetite statement says 'We accept residual risk up to $500K annual loss expectancy per risk'. A newly identified cloud misconfiguration has a 40% likelihood of a $2M breach within 12 months.
practice_questions.text: What is the correct risk management action?
options: Accept the risk — it's within normal business operations
Annualised Loss Expectancy = $800K, exceeding risk appetite — escalate and implement controls to reduce ALE below $500K
Transfer all risk immediately to cyber insurance
Avoid the risk by shutting down the cloud environment
practice_questions.explanation: ALE = 40% × $2M = $800K, exceeding the $500K appetite threshold. This requires risk treatment — implement controls (correct the misconfiguration, add monitoring) to reduce probability or impact so that residual ALE falls within appetite. Insurance is a transfer option but should complement, not replace, primary controls. Avoidance (shutting down cloud) is disproportionate.

labs.title: Enterprise Risk Register Construction
labs.description: Build a complete IT risk register for a mid-size financial institution using FAIR methodology
steps: Step 1: Identify 10 IT risk scenarios from the case study (ransomware, cloud misconfiguration, insider threat, etc.)
Step 2: Assess each risk: threat agent, vulnerability, frequency, impact
Step 3: Apply qualitative scoring (5x5 heat map: Likelihood × Impact)
Step 4: For the top 3 risks, apply FAIR quantitative analysis to calculate ALE
Step 5: Identify existing controls and assess design/operating effectiveness
Step 6: Determine residual risk and compare to risk appetite threshold
Step 7: Document risk acceptance decisions and assign owners
Step 8: Design KRIs for the top 3 risks with monitoring frequency

practice_questions.text: During a control effectiveness review, you find a key IT general control (automated access revocation upon termination) has a 15% failure rate over 12 months. What is the MOST appropriate response?
options: Accept the risk — automated controls are inherently imperfect
Immediately classify as a significant deficiency, implement a compensating detective control (monthly user access review), and prioritise remediation of the root cause
Retrain the HR team responsible for termination processing
Report to external auditors and await their recommendation
practice_questions.explanation: A 15% failure rate in an automated access revocation control is material — terminated employees retaining access represents a significant access control risk. Best practice: (1) classify severity (likely significant deficiency or material weakness depending on impact), (2) implement a compensating control (periodic detective review of terminated-user access) immediately, (3) root-cause analysis — is it a process failure (HR not timely notifying IT), a system integration issue, or both? Remediate the root cause and re-test.

Step 2: Map each goal to 2-3 measurable KPIs
Step 3: Define target, threshold, and alert levels for each KPI
Step 4: Design dashboard layout: executive summary, trend charts, top risks

practice_questions.text: When establishing an IT governance framework, which factor MOST determines the appropriate governance model for an organisation?
options: The size of the IT department
The organisation's strategic objectives, risk appetite, and regulatory environment
The preference of the CIO
The frameworks used by industry peers
practice_questions.explanation: IT governance is not one-size-fits-all. The appropriate model depends on: (1) strategic objectives — how central is IT to value creation? (2) risk appetite — what level of IT risk is acceptable? (3) regulatory environment — are there specific IT governance mandates (SOX, DORA, etc.)? Peer benchmarking is useful but secondary to the organisation's own context.

labs.title: AI Model Inventory & Risk Classification
labs.description: Build an AI model inventory and classify each model by risk level using NIST AI RMF criteria
steps: Step 1: Review 8 AI system descriptions from the provided case study organisation
Step 2: For each system, document: purpose, data inputs/outputs, decision autonomy level, affected populations
Step 3: Apply NIST AI RMF risk characterisation (impact × likelihood matrix)
Step 4: Apply EU AI Act risk tier classification (Unacceptable/High/Limited/Minimal)
Step 5: Prioritise systems for governance review by combined risk score
Step 6: Recommend specific controls for the 3 highest-risk systems
Step 7: Design the AI model registry schema with required metadata fields

labs.title: Model Drift Detection Setup
labs.description: Configure monitoring for data drift and prediction drift using Evidently AI
steps: Step 1: Load baseline (training) and production (30-day) datasets for a credit scoring model
Step 2: Configure Evidently AI DatasetDriftReport for all input features
Step 3: Configure TargetDriftReport for model predictions
Step 4: Run analysis and identify features with statistically significant drift (p-value < 0.05)
Step 5: Interpret drift report: which features drifted? What is the business impact?
Step 6: Define monitoring thresholds and alerting escalation procedures
Step 7: Document drift findings in audit workpaper format with recommended actions

practice_questions.text: A production AI model's prediction distribution shifts significantly 3 months after deployment. What type of risk has materialised and what is the first control response?
options: Data breach — notify affected customers
Model drift — trigger model revalidation protocol and assess business impact
System outage — restart the model serving infrastructure
Training error — re-train from scratch using original data
practice_questions.explanation: A significant shift in prediction distribution is a classic model drift indicator — either data drift (input distribution changed) or concept drift (the real-world relationship the model learned has changed). The first response is to trigger the model revalidation protocol: (1) quantify the drift (Evidently AI, PSI score), (2) assess business impact (are decisions materially affected?), (3) root-cause analysis, (4) determine if recalibration, retraining, or rollback is needed. Do not re-train blindly without understanding the root cause.

practice_questions.text: What is the PRIMARY purpose of an organisational AI model registry?
options: To store model code and weights for backup purposes
To maintain a complete, auditable inventory of all AI systems with their risk classifications, owners, and governance status
To track GPU compute costs
To publish AI model performance metrics to the public
practice_questions.explanation: An AI model registry is the foundation of AI governance — it provides a complete, auditable inventory of all AI systems deployed (or in development). Essential fields include: model ID, business owner, risk classification, data inputs, regulatory applicability, validation status, approval date, and monitoring configuration. Without a registry, governance is impossible — you cannot govern what you cannot see.
